answer.text: Buy a higher strike put and sell a lower strike put with the same expiry — profits from a moderate decline in the underlying. Max profit = (high strike - low strike) - net premium; max loss = net premium paid
Buy two puts at different strikes
Sell a put and simultaneously sell a call
Buy a put and buy the underlying stock simultaneously

explanation.text: **The Logic:** Bear put spread: long K2 put + short K1 put (K2 > K1). Net premium = K2 put premium - K1 put premium (paid). Max profit (at S ≤ K1) = (K2-K1) - net premium. Max loss (at S ≥ K2) = net premium. BEP = K2 - net premium. It's a debit spread for bearish view. **The Trap:** Two puts without selling is just two separate put positions. Selling put + selling call is a short straddle/strangle. Put + stock is a protective put. **Cross-Exam Context:** Bear put spreads work in equity (VIII), currency (I Ch5 — for bearish USDINR view), and IR options. Structure is identical across markets. **Distributor's Pro-Tip:** Bear put spreads are ideal when you're moderately bearish — they cost less than buying a put outright but cap your downside profit. Use when you have a specific price target on the downside.
